title: Telegram links are persisted to local JSON, which can be lost across production restarts or instances

evidence.quote:
## Deploy on Vercel

The easiest way to deploy your Next.js app is to use the [Vercel Platform](https://vercel.com/new?utm_medium=default-template&filter=next.js&utm_source=create-next-app&utm_campaign=create-next-app-readme) from the creators of Next.js.

reasoning: The webhook stores durable notification preferences in notifications.json under process.cwd(). For a Next.js deployment, especially the documented Vercel path, route handlers can run on ephemeral or horizontally scaled server instances, so writes to local disk are not a durable shared data store. A user can successfully link Telegram and then lose that link after a restart, redeploy, instance change, or execution on another instance.
reproduction: Deploy with multiple runtime instances or redeploy after linking a wallet; the file written by one running instance is not guaranteed to exist for the later oracle or route invocation.
recommendation: Move notification preferences to a durable shared store such as the project database, KV, object storage with atomic writes, or another persistent backend used by the oracle. Treat local JSON only as a development fixture.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No tests were included, and there is no deployment/runtime test that verifies links survive restarts or multi-instance execution.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add an integration test around the persistence abstraction that stores a wallet/chat link, constructs a fresh handler/service instance, and verifies the link is still available from the durable store.
minimumFixScope: Replace direct fs persistence in this route with a durable persistence adapter and update any consumers that read notifications.json.
